body_txt: In old times, mapmaking was a fairly imprecise task, due to the lack of advanced technology for exploration purposes. So, to fill great blank areas on the maps, mapmakers used to include textual and/or graphic warnings of the dangers of going into uncharted territory. Such warnings took the form of Sea Serpents , dragons, cannibals and many other mythical and, sometimes, even real creatures. The actual line "Here There Be Dragons" has been found only once , on the 16th-century Lenox Globe , but is too cool to give up. note There may be a second instance, the Ostrich Egg Globe , but its authenticity is debated. Metafictionally, this tendency is explored in fiction with two usual objectives. The first and more obvious is to show that the map is very ancient or simply medieval. Depending on the setting, the map may be contemporary, but displayed in an outdated manner because that's how things still are made. The other is to avoid showing the viewer a dull and realistic map. This is mostly used in video games, to give the world map a more enjoyable presentation. In-universe, maps of this sort also serve as decorative objects to draw a viewer's eye, but are also often explained as serving as a sort of travel guide to note interesting or dangerous entities on the land that they illustrate. In maps describing well-known and familiar lands, this trope manifests chiefly in a tendency towards decorative artwork. Forests are depicted by stands of carefully-drawn trees, interesting local wildlife or people are scattered around the landscape, and cities and landmarks are pointed by stylistically out-of-scale and often very detailed drawings. Monsters, pirates and the like are illustrated where appropriate, but are unlikely to hugely dominate the scene. The more elaborate examples might go as far as to illustrate the tufts of grass on the plains. If the map extends past the edges of the known world, then things take a turn for the ominous. Great banks of clouds and fog are likely to obscure most of the landscape if it isn't simply blank, interspersed with much larger and more intimidating monstrous beasts, whether real or imagined, and the illustrations of known landmarks if and when these are known. These area is also likely to be marked with a big, intimidating label reading " Unknown Lands ", "Unmapped Regions", or, of course, "Here There Are Monsters". Because Everything's Better in Latin , also sometimes seen as its original spelling, Hic sunt dracones . This is a very common feature of the Fantasy World Map . Space-age variants of this trope are also common, especially in Science Fantasy and in settings where Space Is an Ocean , and typically take the form of decorated start charts illustrating the locations of black holes, Space Clouds , Asteroid Thickets , Negative Space Wedgies , hostile alien powers, ravenous space monsters , and the like. Do not mistake this with Here There Were Dragons , which is about the existence of magic (and even dragons) sometime in the past of the setting of a fictional work. For the actual, physical location being marked so, see Unknown Lands . Also not to be confused with the fantasy movie review show by Nash of TGWTG , or the steampunk fantasy web novel by Terry Zilla .
